speaker: interviewer
text: 1995. I'm Sharon Tyler. I'm interviewing Fred Knoller. We're in London, and this is tape six.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we became engaged, or wanted to become engaged, after one month, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Freda spoke to her parents in England. Said, you're mad. How can you? You met him once. You are going to get engaged? Stay where you are. Father came over with two flight tickets for her. You're coming back. I came with her to the airport to pick him up. And people didn't travel. It was 19-- 1940-- 1950. You know, 1950 it was, yes. And he says, I don't want to have any arguments. You're coming back with me to England. So she introduced me to him. We spoke. I took him to my-- my brother's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and introduce him to my brother, Otto. He was a doctor. To my other brother. He's a designer. He's in Florida. And told him my story and this here. And we became quite close. And anyhow, to make a long story short, Freda just didn't want to hear from him. I'm staying. I'm marrying Fred, and that's the situation. And you can do whatever you want. She was one of the first rebels. [LAUGHS] At that time, there were very rarely that the children were rebels. But she was quite determined. And he said, OK. If you-- if I'm here, OK. Let's have a engagement party. We had an engagement party. And we decided, because of these presents from the government of somewhere around $400, $500, we're going to get married on the last day, New Year's Eve, of 1950. The father went back to England. There was only-- as far as Freda was concerned, there was only her aunt and then some of her friends. There was my brother and-- my two brothers were there, present at the-- with their wives, because they got married in the meantime. And there were 22 people at our wedding in a rabbi's home in Brooklyn, not in a hall with music. But we just had a ceremony in a rabbi's home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we made our home in Baltimore. We got an apartment. I was working, earning $60 a week, which wasn't very much. Freda, because of her English accent, which the Americans just adore, found a job as a secretary to a radio disc jockey.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he only wanted her to speak over the-- you know, when he was talking. And she earned-- I think she earned more than me. So we were able to have an apartment, and we lived there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1952, when her parents said, what are you doing there? We miss you. Come over. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't care where I went-- where I lived as long as I was with Freda. And Freda was homesick, naturally. So we decided to break up everything and to go together, and to go to England and see, from there on, what we can do. Well, it was wonderful to see Freda's-- to meet the family. Freda has two sisters and a brother. And it was-- they made me so welcome, so at home. His-- her parents helped us going into business. We opened up a shop-- a retail shop-- and-- selling raincoats, because her father was in the raincoat business. And we got ourselves a home in Edgware.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] started a family. In 1953, Marcia arrived. And then our second daughter, Susie, in 1956. And I don't know. Shall I tell you about what we are doing-- what we were doing? A little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: We started with one shop called the Mac Shop, because we sold Macs, Mackintoshes. Started in Burnt Oak. And I don't know whether you remember. You must be too young for that. But the-- in the 1960s, Biba started, the shops--

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: The shops for the young teenagers, the teenage cult, where about. So we changed the image of our shop. We still sold rainwear, so-- but then we made the back part into a psychedelic boutique. Back part.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we called it-- we made that 'M' from 'The Mac Shop' into a beautiful M logo. And we put, in little letters above, 'Lady M,' underneath 'boutique.' So 'The Mac Shop' with 'Lady M Boutique.'

speaker: interviewee
text: And we did quite well. We opened up another shop. We had several shops. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And our children were born. And-- and he had a very happy life. We even went-- opened up a shop in-- Brent Cross Shopping Centre opened up. We were, the first five years, there. And after five years, we couldn't continue anymore because it was-- they wanted instead of-- four times the amount of rent. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At one time, we had six shops. But we sold some of them, went out, sold. And somehow, [SIGHS] in 1988, I think-- '87-- we got rid of our last shop. I joined-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] yeah. I-- I was asked-- I didn't-- when we only had one shop, there wasn't enough work for me to do, because my daughter, Marcia, and my wife was running the shops together with some staff. That shop, the last shop we had. So they said, come on, find yourself a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time, somebody asked me to join State of Israel Bonds, which is a government organization to promote investments in Israel-- government shares, stocks, bonds. And this is what I started to do in 1984. And I'm still, today, working there. I'm a director of that company, and I'm still working there today. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think this is more or less-- What are your children doing now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My children-- Marcia is still in the fashion business. She's working for a wholesale firm in London-- central London. Susie traveled to Israel when she was 18 years of age, and she loved it so much. She first started to go on holiday on the kibbutz. Then she wanted to go back, and again she went back. And she now lives in Tel Aviv, working for Scandinavian Airline-- Airlines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are either of your daughters-- are they married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both were married. Both were married to Israelis. Susie got married to a Yemenite boy. It didn't last very long. They were too different backgrounds. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It lasted one and a half years. Marcia married an Israeli who was rather-- he didn't want to work. He wanted her to work and didn't want to work. It lasted two years. Both have no children. Though-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they are happy at the present time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Marcia has her own beautiful flat in Hampstead, and Susie has a lovely apartment in Tel Aviv, a garden flat. And it's very nice. We see them very often because, through Susie, we are getting cheap flight tickets to see her. And very often she comes to London. And very often I go to Israel on business with State of Israel Bonds.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think back of all that you've been through and your personal sort of journey, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is there anything particular that stands out that you feel you want to sort of leave as a message?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I leave this-- my message, really, is don't ever despair. People who are not an optimist, I feel pity for them because even today, of-- throughout my whole life, I've felt that you can only succeed in life if your attitude is an optimistic one. If you give up, you're a goner. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's-- this is-- I think this is what saved, also, my life-- my attitude towards things. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Otherwise, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I have-- what can I say? I think people take things very much to heart, and it hurts-- can-- can hurt. I only started talking about it-- I didn't talk to my whole family not until 10 years ago, I started talking about it. But-- What was the turning point for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The turning point was when Marcia said to me, Daddy, look. We are grown up now. We don't know anything about you. We don't know anything. We know you have brothers. We don't know anything about your family in Vienna, your life in Vienna, about grandparents. We don't know anything. We don't know what you went through during the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You must tell us because how can we not tell our children if they ask about Grandpa. And this made me think, and I started to tell them. But funnily enough, by talking about it, it was therapy for me. It was a tremendous therapy. So much so that I've decided I want to talk-- tell the world about it. I spoke-- I'm speaking, even today, to school children. I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm just in the process of writing my memoirs. I want the world to know what has happened. And I must say I'm very proud to be on your tapes, because the worked that Steven Spielberg is doing is so very important for the future-- for the future generations. Because they will be able to see what has happened from the horse's mouth, to a certain extent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we finish, I just want to ask you about your-- your Jewish faith, kind of what's happened to that over the years. Has that changed? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I must say, ever since 1948, when Israel was born, a change came into me. I had a pride. I became proud to be a Jew. Not a religious Jew. I'm still going, like my father, twice a year to synagogues. But whereas before, in Vienna, I was afraid-- I didn't want to show that I was Jewish-- I suddenly, when Israel was born, I had a pride. Look what we have achieved. Look what we are achieving all the time. And whenever-- [LAUGHS] I remember, I went to-- on holidays with my children, and to Italy and the beach, and there were always Germans there. And I made sure I always wear a Magen David, a gold chain Magen David. And I made sure that I'm wearing it when I go on the beach, because I want people to know, here is a Jew. And I'm proud of it. And whenever somebody asks me anything, I tell them, I'm a Jew. I once went-- when we went, a year ago, for the first time to Vienna, back to where I was born. Freda went with me, and we went with another couple. And [SIGHS] we went to the apartment house where we lived. And behind us, there were another couple coming in with two children. And they were asking us, oh, are you looking for something, in German. I said, yes. Not looking for something. I used to live here. I left here in '38 because I was a Jew, and Hitler didn't want me. And I wanted to go up into the flat where I lived and see whether, you know, maybe bring back memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said, do me a favor. When you come up-- when you come down-- we're on the second floor-- come down, have [INAUDIBLE] with us. Have a drink with us. And I must say, they were young-- a young couple, because the children were quite small.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but I made it my business to tell them, I had to leave. I was a Jew, and I'm still a Jew, and I'm proud to be a Jew. And they wanted to ask-- they were very nice. They asked me in front of their children. They wanted to the children to know what Hitler has done and all this here. He was a teacher. They were both teachers actually. It was-- they are what I call good people. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It was in German. You spoke to them in German?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke to them in-- no, I spoke to them in English. They spoke English. But for the children's sake, they asked me questions in German, I asked-- I spoke in German. But I still hesitate to speak in German. I still hesitate. When we go on holiday, I never speak on-- in German. I always speak in-- in English or in French.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to finish with? Do you have anything else to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't know. I think I've said enough. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mr. Knoller.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before I ask you to introduce Freda, please tell me what happened to your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: I only found out later on. In fact, I got documentations of what actually happened. They were deported from Vienna to Theresienstadt. They were deported on the 2nd of October, 1942-- 1942 to Theresienstadt, both of them-- both of them, my mother and father, the same day. So I'm glad to see that they were together. And then they say that the date of transfer to-- from Theresienstadt, destination Auschwitz, was the 19th of October, 1944. Now, if you remember, Auschwitz was liberated in January 1945. So this is only three-- October, November, December-- about three months before the liberation. It's tragic. Who knows that this was the last of the people who were gassed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] This is about my parents. Now may I introduce you to Freda Knoller, my wife. We are now married-- 31st of December will be the 45th anniversary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mazel tov.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we got married 1950. It's a long time, poor girl, you had to suffer. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think I'm quite well on it really. [LAUGHS] I would do it again.

speaker: interviewee
text: You would?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's a compliment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it like being married to a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew very little about his story until about 10 years ago. Of course, when we met, he told me he had been in camp, and various other people he knew said the same thing. But no information came out from any of them, just like in passing, you know, in conversation. But about 10 years ago, he really began talking. And I'm pleased he did because I think he is a happier person for having done that. It has come out. You know, before that, there were times when I used to just wonder why he thought along certain lines. Sometimes he seemed illogical. And I understand now. It-- you know, his background did change him. But I'm happy he is doing what he's doing. He's speaking. He's not just speaking to you. He speaks to groups of school children. And the wonderful thing is-- and I think it's done him a lot of good-- is speaking to his Christian groups. And he's made a lot of friends. And he now knows [LAUGHS] that there are some good people in the world as well. You know, it has-- it has mellowed him to know that we have friends. You know, at one time, he was so bitter. But a lot of that has gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Tell me about this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo taken-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as you can see, [LAUGHS] the middle one, that's me, the baby, with my two brothers on either side. My parents in the chair-- deck chair. This was taken on holiday. I do not know when it was. I have taken this copy, actually, from albums of my brother's because, as you can really imagine, I didn't have photos. As earlier-- I didn't have anything to take with me from Vienna. But this was a copy taken from my brother's photo collection.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is-- as you can see, we were already much more grown up. I think it was 1937. It was before the-- Hitler came into-- into Austria. You see my parents. On the left-- on the left side is my mother. In the middle, my father. And on the right-hand side is our very good friend and neighbor Erna Schiff. She actually was instrumental to bring my brother, Eric, to Florida, through her cousin, and also to introduce me to another cousin in Antwerp. And she was a very good friend of our family. Eric with the violin, Otto on the piano, me with the cello. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the back-- this was taken in our living room, with the grandfather clock in the middle. And the windows had these beautiful lace curtains. It was a lovely house. It was-- was very, very well-cared for, and it was really a home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a piece of my jacket that I cut up, because I didn't want to keep the whole jacket and the trousers-- striped trousers-- for future references. But I thought that I am going to use this just as a souvenir. As you can see, there's a red triangle with an F on it, which is the sign of a political French prisoner. Political, not as a Jew. What actually happened during the death march from Auschwitz, one of the people who died collapsed on the road. It's-- actually, he collapsed in the-- in the brick factory where we rested. He was dead. I took his triangle off his body, and stuck it on my-- underneath my number and over the Star of David, which I had there, because somehow I felt, if I can say that I'm not a Jew-- that I'm a political prisoner-- that I may survive better. And I kept it on until I-- now as a souvenir. That was my number-- 157103.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] When I went to Dr. [? Bentain ?] in that little village in central France to recuperate, he asked me to put on my striped pajama and gave me a shovel, and he took a photo. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As you can see, my hair was al-- still very short, because it must have been somewhere around maybe two months after liberation. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Oh, this is the happy couple, Freda and Freddy, when we got married, 31st of December, 1950. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You can see how young I was, and Freda. Although I must say, Freda even looks lovelier today than she was then. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a photo of Professor Weis, my benefactor who, I believe, saved my life for letting me eat double the amount to anybody in Auschwitz. I loved this man. Unfortunately, he died in Paris in 1972. I believe it was 1972. He was a wonderful, wonderful person. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There you see Marcia in one of our shops with a beautiful dress. She always likes to pose in dresses. She's a beautiful young lady, as you can see. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Terrific, fun-loving girl. I love her very much. We both, Freda and I, we love her very much. That's Marcia Knoller.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our Israeli daughter, Susie. You see her here in a uniform of-- at her job at Scandinavian Airlines in Israel. She is tremendous. She's fun. She loves life. She's a terrific person. We are very, very proud of her. Susie Knoller.
